name: Oganesson
name_origin: Named in honor of Russian nuclear physicist Yuri Oganessian, leader of the JINR Dubna team that pioneered the synthesis of many superheavy elements; one of only two elements named after a living person at the time of naming.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna team led by Yuri Oganessian, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

occurrence.abundance_summary: Oganesson does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via the hot-fusion reaction 48Ca + 249Cf, with only a handful of atoms ever observed.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: Oganesson is formally placed in group 18 below the noble gases, but relativistic calculations predict it should be significantly more reactive than xenon or radon and may even be a solid or semiconductor at standard conditions, with a relatively diffuse electron density that could permit a stable +2 or +4 oxidation state. No chemistry has been experimentally measured given its extreme rarity and short-lived nuclei.
isotopes_summary: Only one isotope of oganesson, 294Og, has been confirmed. It has a half-life of about 0.7 milliseconds and decays by alpha emission to livermorium. Fewer than ten atoms have been reliably observed worldwide.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Oganesson
Jerabek, P. et al. 'Electron and Nucleon Localization Functions of Oganesson', Phys. Rev. Lett., 2018
